methods.description: This method details the laboratory procedures for extracting, purifying, identifying, quantifying, and performing compound-specific stable isotope analysis (δ²H for n-alkanes, δ¹⁸O for sugars) on samples from the Gemündener Maar GM1 core.

steps.description: For δ 2 H analyses of n-alkanes as well as δ 18 O analyses of sugars, 59 samples were prepared from 607 cm to 694 cm depth of the Gemündener Maar GM1 core, in order to cover the core section with already high TOC content and the Late Glacial to Holocene transition.
steps.searchable_summary: Select specific samples from core based on depth range for biomarker and isotope analysis.

steps.description: n-Alkanes were extracted from 1-6 g freeze-dried and grinded samples...
steps.searchable_summary: Freeze-dry and grind selected samples.

steps.description: ...by microwave extraction at 100°C for 1 h, using 15 ml of solvent (dichloromethane and methanol, in a ratio 9:1).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform microwave extraction of n-alkanes using dichloromethane/methanol solvent.

steps.description: The resultant total lipid extracts were separated over aminopropyl silica gel (Supelco 45 µm) filled pipette columns. Nonpolar compounds (including n-alkanes) were eluted with n-hexane.
steps.searchable_summary: Separate total lipid extract using aminopropyl silica gel column chromatography with n-hexane elution.

steps.description: The fraction was spiked with a known amount of 5α-androstanone, used as internal standard.
steps.searchable_summary: Spike nonpolar compound fraction with 5α-androstanone internal standard.

steps.description: Identification and quantification was carried out on an Agilent MS 5975 (EI) interfaced with an Agilent 7890 GC equipped with a 30 m fused silica capillary column (HP5-MS 0.25 mm i.d., 0.25 µm film thickness), and a split/splitless injector operating in splitless mode at 320°C. Carrier gas was helium and the temperature program was 1 min at 50°C, from 50 to 200°C at 30°C min -1 , from 200 to 320°C at 7°C min -1 , 5 min at 320°C. Data recording comprised the total ion count (scan mode from m/z 40 to m/z 600) and single ion monitoring (m/z 57, 71, 85 and 99).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform GC-MS analysis for compound identification and quantification using specified parameters.

steps.description: Concentrations were calculated relative to the internal standard and to an external standard (n-C21 to n-C40 alkane mixture, Supelco), injected in different concentrations (40 ng/µl, 4 ng/µl, 1 ng/µl, 0.4 ng/µl).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate compound concentrations using internal and external standards.

steps.description: Prior to compound-specific isotope analyses, the n-alkanes were further purified. The nonpolar fractions were passed over a pipette column filled with activated AgNO3 impregnated silica gel...
steps.searchable_summary: Purify n-alkanes using activated AgNO3 impregnated silica gel column.

steps.description: ...and a pipette column filled with zeolite (Geokleen).
steps.searchable_summary: Purify n-alkanes using zeolite column.

steps.description: After drying, the zeolite was removed using hydrofluoric acid...
steps.searchable_summary: Remove zeolite from sample using hydrofluoric acid after drying.

steps.description: ...and the n-alkanes were recovered by liquid-liquid extraction with hexane.
steps.searchable_summary: Recover n-alkanes using liquid-liquid extraction with hexane.

steps.description: The purified n-alkane fractions were measured for their compound-specific stable hydrogen isotope composition (δ²H). The measurements were performed at the Institute of Geography, University of Bern on an IsoPrime 100 IRMS, coupled to an Agilent 7890A GC via a GC5 pyrolysis/combustion interface operating in pyrolysis modus with a Cr (ChromeHD) reactor at 1000°C. Samples were injected with a split/splitless injector. The GC was equipped with 30 m fused silica column (HP5-MS, 0.32 mm inner diameter, 0.25 µm film thickness).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform compound-specific δ²H isotope analysis using GC-IRMS with pyrolysis.
outputs: Raw isotope ratio data (e.g., H3+ beam, m/z 2/3 signals)

steps.description: The precision was checked by co-analyzing a standard alkane mixture (n-C27, n-C29, n-C33) with known isotope composition (A. Schimmelmann, University of Indiana), injected twice every six runs.
steps.searchable_summary: Check analytical precision of δ²H measurements using known isotope standard mixture.
inputs: Standard alkane mixture (n-C27, n-C29, n-C33) with known δ²H

steps.description: The samples were analyzed in threefold repetitions (except from the samples in 622 and 672 cm depth), and the analytical precision was generally better than 5‰.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze samples in triplicate for δ²H reproducibility.

steps.description: The H3 + -correction factor was checked every two days and stayed stable over the course of measurements at 3.14.
steps.searchable_summary: Check and monitor H3+ correction factor for IRMS δ²H measurements.

steps.description: The stable hydrogen isotope compositions are given in the δ-notation (δ 2 Hn-alkane) versus Vienna Standard Mean Ocean Water (VSMOW).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate δ²H values relative to VSMOW standard.

steps.description: The δ 2 Hn-alkane values refer to the area weighted mean of the δ 2 H values of n-alkanes with 27 and 29 carbon atoms (n-C27, n-C29), respectively, because of their relatively high abundance in the samples.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate area-weighted mean δ²H for n-C27 and n-C29 n-alkanes.
inputs: δ²H values for individual n-alkanes (n-C27, n-C29)
outputs: Area-weighted mean δ²Hn-alkane value per sample

steps.description: The sample preparation for δ 18 O analyses of hemicellulose/polysaccharide-derived sugars followed standard procedures at the Institute of Agronomy and Nutritional Sciences, Soil Biogeochemistry, Martin-Luther-University Halle-Wittenberg, according to the method of Zech and Glaser (2009). The monosaccharide sugars were hydrolytically extracted from samples containing approximately 10 mg total organic carbon...
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare samples for sugar extraction and δ¹⁸O analysis following Zech and Glaser (2009).
outputs: Prepared samples for sugar extraction (normalized by TOC)

steps.description: ...with 10 ml of 4 M trifluoroacetic acid at 105°C for 4 h, applying the method described by Amelung et al. (1996).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform hydrolytic extraction of sugars using trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) at elevated temperature.

steps.description: After filtration over glass fibre filters...
steps.searchable_summary: Filter sugar extract using glass fibre filters.

steps.description: ...the extracted sugars were cleaned using XAD-7 (to remove humic-like substances)...
steps.searchable_summary: Clean sugar extract using XAD-7 resin to remove humic substances.
outputs: Sugar extract cleaned from humic substances

steps.description: ...and Dowex 50WX8 columns (to remove interfering cations).
steps.searchable_summary: Clean sugar extract using Dowex 50WX8 resin to remove cations.
inputs: Sugar extract cleaned from humic substances

steps.description: Afterwards, the purified samples were freeze-dried...
steps.searchable_summary: Freeze-dry purified sugar extract.

steps.description: ...and derivatized by adding methylboronic acid (4 mg in 400 µl pyridine) for 1 h at 60°C.
steps.searchable_summary: Derivatize dried sugar extract using methylboronic acid.

steps.description: δ 18 O analyses of hemicellulose/polysaccharide-derived sugars...
steps.searchable_summary: Perform compound-specific δ¹⁸O isotope analysis of derivatized sugars using GC-IRMS (implied).

steps.description: (Implied calculation vs VSMOW, standard for oxygen isotopes)
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate δ¹⁸O values relative to VSMOW standard.

steps.description: The δ 18 Osugar values refer to the area weighted mean of the δ 18 O values of arabinose, fucose, xylose, mannose, galactose and glucose. Rhamnose areas, respectively concentrations, were too low for reliable isotope measurements in most samples.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate area-weighted mean δ¹⁸O for specific sugars (arabinose, fucose, xylose, mannose, galactose, glucose).
outputs: Area-weighted mean δ¹⁸Osugar value per sample

methods.description: This method describes the process of reconstructing relative humidity (RHdv) based on the coupled δ²Hn-alkane and δ¹⁸Osugar paleohygrometer approach, including calculations of leaf water isotope values (δl, dexcess) and applying correction scenarios.

steps.description: The biomarker-based leaf water values (δl = δ 2 Hl, δ 18 Ol via Eq. 8 and 9)...
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate leaf water δ²Hl and δ¹⁸Ol using specified equations (Eq. 8 and 9).

steps.description: ...result in dexcess values of leaf water (dl) ranging between -125 and -30‰.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate leaf water d-excess (dl) from calculated δ²Hl and δ¹⁸Ol values.

steps.description: The difference between the d-excesses of the leaf water and source water can serve as proxy for mean daytime vegetation period relative humidity (RHdv; red double arrow).
steps.searchable_summary: Reconstruct mean daytime vegetation period relative humidity (RHdv) using leaf water d-excess and source water d-excess.

steps.description: By contrast, when only correcting for the 2 H signal dampening (scenario 2 in Tab. 1), this leads to 0.0 to 10.6‰ more positive dl* and 0.0 to 5.6% higher RHdv values (dl* and RHdv* in Fig. 6A and B).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply correction scenario 2 (²H dampening) to calculate corrected dl* and RHdv* values.
inputs: Correction parameters for ²H dampening (from Tab. 1, not provided)

steps.description: When only correcting for the 18 O signal dampening (scenario 3 in Tab. 1), dl values decrease by 0.0 to -22.7‰, corresponding to RH decreases of 0.0 to -12.0% (dl # and RHdv # in Fig. 6A and B, respectively).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply correction scenario 3 (¹⁸O dampening) to calculate corrected dl# and RHdv# values.
inputs: Correction parameters for ¹⁸O dampening (from Tab. 1, not provided)

steps.description: Accordingly, the full correction for grass-derived alkane and sugar biomarkers (scenario 4 in Tab. 1) results in 0.0 to 6.3% (mean 1.8%) lower RHdv values (RHdv # * in Fig. 6B). This corresponds to dl decreases of 0.0 to -12.0‰ (dl # * in Fig. 6A).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply correction scenario 4 (full dampening correction) to calculate corrected dl*# and RHdv*# values.
inputs: Correction parameters for full dampening (from Tab. 1, not provided)

steps.description: For comparison, the modern RHdv value (6 a.m. to 7 p.m. from April to October) from the adjacent meteorological station Nürburg-Barweiler (approx. 25 km northeast of Lake GM; hourly data from 1995 to 2015 from Deutscher Wetterdienst, 2016) is 67% (Fig. 6B). In addition, the range of the reconstructed RHdv values of 50% is well in agreement with the modern RHdv variability of 45%, within a range of 48% to 93% (definition and meteorological station details as above).
steps.searchable_summary: Compare reconstructed RHdv time series with modern meteorological RH data.
outputs: Comparison results (e.g., mean difference, range comparison)

steps.description: In order to test the robustness of this assumption and respective effects on reconstructed RH values, we used data of Jacob and Sonntag (1991), who measured the isotope composition of precipitation and of atmospheric water vapour in Heidelberg, Germany, during the period 1981 to 1989.
steps.searchable_summary: Fetch/use external isotope data (precipitation, vapor) from Jacob and Sonntag (1991).
outputs: External isotope data (precipitation δ²H/δ¹⁸O, vapor δ²H/δ¹⁸O)

steps.description: The derived apparent fractionation (ap) amounts to 18.3 and 1.57‰ in average for 2 H and 18 O, respectively. We used this ap in Eq. (1) instead of the difference δa -δs and recalculated the RH values. This recalculation leads to an average RH change of only -1.7% (±0.9), which is far below the analytical errors of the d-excess of leaf water.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate apparent fractionation (εap) from external data and recalculate RH values using εap in Eq. (1).
inputs: Calculated εap values (18.3‰ for ²H, 1.57‰ for ¹⁸O)

steps.description: Finally, the stability of the d-excess and slope of the LMWLGermany through the past needs to be discussed. According to Stumpp et al. (2014), the long-term d-excess of precipitation from 28 sites in Germany does not show pronounced relationships to local climate conditions of the site. All reported values are close to 10‰... In addition, the Europe suggest that the atmospheric circulation patterns over the European continent were rather constant throughout the past 35,000 years... In summary, the variations in the slope of the LMWL of Germany are assumed to be rather small over longer timescales.
steps.searchable_summary: Assess stability of LMWL d-excess and slope over time based on literature review.
inputs: Literature findings (Stumpp et al., 2014; Rozanski et al., 1993; Rozanski, 1985) regarding LMWL in Germany/Europe
